sentences.text: My name is James Krauss, K-R-A-U-S-S.
My birth was Imre Krauss, K-R-A-U-S-S.
I was born May 2, 1930, in Budapest 19-- yeah, 1930.
I would say life was fairly normal, considering, before the war.
We-- we saw-- we had the usual difficulties, which we didn't consider that difficult.
Oh, occasionally you got beat up.
But it was not that difficult.
But we-- we really didn't think that this was that unusual.
My father's name was Bela Krauss, and he was in business.
My mother's name was Bertha Krauss, and she was a housewife.
My mother died in 1935, and things changed, obviously.
And my-- my father-- [PAUSES] my father-- [PAUSES] my father was the-- my father became the principal provider.
And-- [PAUSES] so he was the principal provider, and--
No, I stayed with my father and my aunt.
Yes, they had children, my cousin.
Micky. ?] And he was like my brother.
And-- he was like my brother.
It was-- it was a very comfortable apartment.
And it was a cozy apartment.
Well, it was-- it was a cozy apartment.
It was-- it was-- it was a cozy apartment [LAUGHS].
I remember it had about seven rooms.
Well, we are talking about another apartment.
It was-- [PAUSES] trying to describe it.
[PAUSES] What color, did you ask me?
It was like a light-- light-- light-- [PAUSES] it was like a light beige.
And-- [PAUSES] it was a nice, cozy-- here I go again.
But it was a cozy, cozy apartment.
Both my aunts-- both my aunts, they were great readers.
And I was a-- I was a good reader also.
And-- [PAUSES] I remember I was-- I was reading a lot even as a child.
I remember I was reading-- trying to think the books I was reading at that early age.

But it wasn't-- it wasn't-- [PAUSES] it was not-- even though we had religious books, it wasn't the central part, even though that religion was an important part of-- but it was not-- can I have something to drink?
It was an important part of life but wasn't the central part of life.

We went to the Orthodox temple in my neighborhood.
Oh, a typical Shabbos was very warm.
Typical Shabbos was-- [PAUSES] well, it was, as I said, very warm.

and we had-- we had-- [PAUSES] we had fish.

Well, the Germans came in, took over officially.
Day to day, every day there was a new rule-- rule or regulation.

It was rules-- rules and regulations were coming in as far as-- as far as-- [PAUSES] there were rules to the extent of-- it became like-- anti-Jewish laws became constant-- constant-- constant harassment.
And so it was constant-- constant harassment.
